topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick and suffocating, clinging to my skin like cheap perfume. I’d been trying to appear composed, a mask of polite neutrality, but every time my eyes flickered toward the entrance, a knot tightened painfully in my chest. Then, there he was. Liam. Standing there, beaming, his arm linked with some dazzlingly happy stranger. My jaw clenched so tight I could feel the muscles protesting. I watched him for a full, agonizing thirty seconds, the smile on his face seeming to mock every past argument we’d ever had. When he finally turned, our gazes collided across the crowded room. The forced pleasantries we exchanged were brittle, sharp little shards of ice. I managed a tight nod, my expression carefully blank, but the heat rising in my cheeks betrayed the simmering frustration churning beneath the surface. I needed air. I needed distance.

[story 2]

I kept my hands jammed deep into my pockets, anchoring myself to the reality of the champagne flute in my hand. This entire event was an endurance test, and every glance toward the entrance felt like a deliberate assault. When I saw her—Sarah—standing near the buffet, laughing easily with the groom’s cousin, a sudden, unwelcome jolt shot through me. My knuckles whitened around the glass. I felt a distinct urge to pivot, to walk directly into the nearest exit and disappear into the anonymity of the parking lot. When she finally looked up, her eyes met mine for a fraction of a second. There was a flicker of something—recognition, maybe regret—that made my stomach twist with a bitter resentment. I offered nothing, just a stiff, dismissive glance before turning my attention back to the dance floor, forcing a smile that felt like tearing paper.

[story 3]

The music seemed too loud, the laughter too saccharine. I was leaning against a pillar, trying to make myself small, trying to dissolve into the ornate wallpaper. Then, the ambient noise seemed to drop out, replaced by a sharp, unwelcome clarity when I saw him. Marcus. He was laughing, genuinely delighted, and he was standing near the entryway. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged intake of air. Every nerve ending seemed to tighten simultaneously. I could feel the blood pressure spiking in my temples. When his eyes swept the room and landed on mine, there was a moment of absolute stillness between us, a silent acknowledgment of everything unspoken. I didn't move. I just held his gaze, letting the sheer weight of my displeasure settle into my posture—shoulders squared, a rigid set to my spine that screamed volumes louder than any shouted accusation.
